results.possibleIdentity: Gary Stephan (born 1942) is an American abstract painter, sculptor, and printmaker who became a prominent figure in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Brooklyn, he studied at the Parsons School of Design and Pratt Institute before moving to San Francisco, where he earned his M.F.A. from the San Francisco Art Institute in 1967. After returning to New York, he served as a studio assistant to the artist Jasper Johns. Stephan's work was featured in the Whitney Biennial in 1971 and 1973, and he has been associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), appearing in exhibitions chronicling the organization's history. Known for his rigorous formalism and idiosyncratic abstract style, his work explores the destabilization of figure-ground relationships and negative space. Over his career, he has been represented by influential galleries such as the Bykert Gallery, Mary Boone Gallery, and Susan Inglett Gallery, and his work is held in the permanent collections of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), and the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum. He also serves on the faculty of the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York City.

results.possibleIdentity: Lisa Zerra was an individual active in art circles during the early 1960s, notably serving as the Treasurer of the Art Club at Agawam High School (Class of 1963) in Massachusetts. During this time, the Art Club focused on stimulating interest in creative and expressive phases of art and acquainting members with cultural contributions. While she is not widely documented as a professional artist in major archives, her background in art education aligns with the timeframe of being an aspiring artist or art enthusiast in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She appears to be part of a family with strong artistic ties; her peer (and likely relative) Antonia Zerra was described as a 'terrific artist' and an 'accomplished art student' in the same 1963 yearbook. Additionally, Robert (Rob) Zera, who might be related, was a Pratt Institute-trained industrial designer and visual artist active from the early 1970s in New York, where he worked at various design firms. The surname Zerra is sometimes used interchangeably with Serra or Zera in historical records.

results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Rosemary Beresford (ONZM) is a New Zealand-based academic, pharmacist, and theatre practitioner who has been active in the arts and sciences since at least the late 1960s. She is best known in the arts community as a long-term committee member and "stalwart" of the Globe Theatre in Dunedin, New Zealand, which was founded in 1961 and noted for its experimental and avant-garde productions during the 1960s and 1970s. Beresford led a significant fundraising campaign that raised over $500,000 for the restoration of the Globe Theatre's heritage building. Professionally, she was an academic at the University of Otago's School of Pharmacy, joining in 1982 and serving as Associate Dean before her retirement in 2008. Her work frequently bridged the gap between science and the humanities; she has published research on Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and computer modeling, and has authored or edited works on the history of medicine, traditional remedies, and local history (such as the Harwood Township). Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) likely stems from her dual involvement in experimental theatre and technological/scientific research, aligning with E.A.T.'s mission to promote collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists.

Rosemary Beresford (1916–2005) was a British educationist and literary reviewer who was active in the 1960s and 1970s. She served as an assistant senior education officer at the BBC and was the principal of Sidney Webb College from 1961 until its closure in 1975. During her tenure, she focused on training mature students for the teaching profession. Following her retirement, she remained active in the academic community, serving as a pro-rector at the institution that became the University of Westminster and contributing literary reviews to The Times Higher Education Supplement. While primarily known for her work in education, her role at the BBC during the 1960s and her work as a reviewer of humanities and art-related subjects place her within the historical artistic and intellectual milieu of that period.
